title: var
body:
## Definition

**var** is the legacy keyword for declaring a [function-scoped](concept:function-scope) binding with [hoisting](concept:hoisting) initialized to [undefined](concept:undefined) â€” no [temporal dead zone](concept:temporal-dead-zone).

## Why it exists

Original JS variable declaration before `let`/`const` (ES6). Still appears in legacy code and generated output.

## How it works

- Hoisted to top of function/global with value `undefined`.
- Allows redeclaration in same scope.
- Loop variable is shared across iterations (one binding).

## Example

```js
console.log(count); // undefined
var count = 1;

for (var i = 0; i < 3; i++) {
  setTimeout(() => console.log(i), 0); // 3, 3, 3
}
```

## Common confusion

`var` at top level creates global property on `window` in browsers; `let`/`const` do not. Prefer `const`/`let` in all modern code.

## In practice

Interview contrast with `let`/`const` is about scope, TDZ, and loop closure behavior.
bodyLinked:
## Definition

**[var](concept:var)** is the legacy keyword for declaring a [function-scoped](concept:function-scope) [binding](concept:binding) with [hoisting](concept:hoisting) initialized to [undefined](concept:undefined) â€” no [temporal dead zone](concept:temporal-dead-zone).

## Why it exists

Original JS variable declaration before [let](concept:let)/[const](concept:const) (ES6). Still appears in legacy code and generated output.

## How it works

- [Hoisted](concept:hoisting) to top of function/global with value [undefined](concept:undefined).
- Allows redeclaration in same scope.
- Loop variable is shared across [iterations](concept:iterator) (one [binding](concept:binding)).

## Example

```js
console.log(count); // undefined
var count = 1;

for (var i = 0; i < 3; i++) {
  setTimeout(() => console.log(i), 0); // 3, 3, 3
}
```

## Common confusion

[var](concept:var) at top level creates global property on `window` in browsers; [let](concept:let)/[const](concept:const) do not. Prefer [const](concept:const)/[let](concept:let) in all modern code.

## In practice

Interview contrast with [let](concept:let)/[const](concept:const) is about scope, [TDZ](concept:temporal-dead-zone), and loop [closure](concept:closure) behavior.
